chapters.title: Science and technology have reshaped humanity's oldest threats
chapters.text:
Humans climbed to the top of the food chain, yet for most of history survival was precarious. Famine, poverty, and war repeatedly pushed civilizations toward collapse — and those topics still dominate headlines. Harari argues that while our problems feel unprecedented, we have become remarkably skilled at neutralizing the catastrophes that once defined human existence.

We do not become satisfied by leading a peaceful and prosperous existence. Instead, we become happy when reality matches our expectations. The bad news is that as conditions improve, expectations balloon. ~ Prof. Yuval Noah Harari

Where earlier generations blamed divine punishment for chaos, modern societies increasingly treat disasters as solvable engineering problems. We study failures, build preventive infrastructure, and pivot quickly toward the next perceived existential risk.

Technology has amplified that capacity — containing outbreaks, averting starvation in many regions, and reducing battlefield casualties relative to historical norms. The success, however, leaves a void: what do highly capable, well-equipped humans pursue once the classic apocalypses lose their grip?

Harari suggests the next evolutionary chapter will center on happiness, immortality, and godlike power — ambitions that sound fantastical today but already shape research budgets and cultural imagination.

chapters.title: Immortality, happiness, and divinity will drive the next era
chapters.text:
Future humanity may organize itself around three pursuits: defeating death, engineering contentment, and ascending toward divine capability. Foundations are already visible. Patients receive exhaustive health disclosures; billions fund pharmaceuticals, nanotechnology, and biotech aimed at extending life.

Treatments that once provoked moral panic now attract investment from technology giants openly targeting mortality itself. Global life expectancy climbed from roughly forty during eras of plague and famine to about seventy by the twentieth century. Eliminating death entirely remains unlikely — accidents and suicide will persist — but compressing disease and aging is a live scientific program.

The happiness quest has grown similarly obsessive. Researchers move beyond biochemical mood drugs toward direct neural stimulation, genetic tuning of pleasure pathways, and algorithmic mood management. Antidepressants foreshadow a broader industry of psychological enhancement.

Together, immortality research and happiness engineering point toward a being unburdened by pain, fear, and ordinary human limits — what Harari labels the shift from Homo sapiens to Homo deus. This is not biblical omnipotence but mythic stature: Zeus-like command over environment, creativity, and mortality.

Merging biology with machines will rewrite genes, deploy intelligent robots, and compress evolutionary timelines in ways history has never witnessed.

chapters.title: Domestication rewrote the bond between humans, animals, and ecosystems
chapters.text:
Human dominance over the planet invites a historical audit. Religious creation stories once placed humanity at the center of a divinely ordered world. Science later supplied a different confidence — the power to reshape nature deliberately.

Archeology shows hunter-gatherer phases preceding agriculture, contradicting some scriptural timelines. In animist cultures, humans maintained reciprocal relationships with plants and animals, acknowledging their role in ecological balance.

The agricultural revolution, reinforced by major religions, elevated gods and humans while demoting animals to soulless resources existing mainly for food and ritual sacrifice.

Science gave humans the power and confidence to make changes.

A subsequent era elevated humans toward godlike authority over the biosphere. Domestication now defines how we relate to other species — and the numbers are stark. Farmed animals vastly outnumber wild populations; humanity has altered ecology faster than asteroid strikes or plate tectonics, driving extinctions and climate disruption.

Our task must be to free ourselves by widening our circle of compassion to embrace all living creatures and the whole of nature and its beauty. ~ Albert Einstein

Much of that hierarchy rests on the belief that only humans possess souls worthy of an afterlife while animals dissolve into nothingness — a claim Harari treats as scientifically shaky. Animals likely experience consciousness in forms we barely understand.

chapters.title: Shared myths are the scaffolding of modern civilization
chapters.text:
Roughly seventy thousand years ago, humans discovered that collective imagination could coordinate large groups. Stories, taboos, and shared beliefs kept tribes aligned and willing to sacrifice for communal values.

Hunting and gathering alone could not sustain cities, but agriculture unlocked scale. Ancient Egypt illustrated the pattern: pharaohs symbolized power, yet bureaucrats and priests sustained the myth that made obedience feel natural.

Imagination is the only weapon in the war against reality. ~ Lewis Carroll

Compelling myths remain the glue for any mass collaboration — nations, corporations, currencies. Writing amplified the effect by fixing narratives in scripture and law, shaping behavior across millennia.

Religion evolves with social needs, supplying moral order even as scientific narratives compete for authority. Science prioritizes testable facts and technical solutions to famine, plague, and war; religion organizes meaning and conduct. Both have propelled humanity forward and will continue influencing pivotal choices.

chapters.title: Economic growth reshapes — and strains — the natural world
chapters.text:
The modern economy thrives on the belief that knowledge and innovation can generate new wealth indefinitely. Premodern societies often assumed a fixed pool of resources; survival meant extracting maximum value from what already existed, a worldview religions reinforced by warning that greed invites ruin.

Modernity generally revolves around a notion that humankind, through knowledge and ideas, can create new resources and grow its wealth.

That optimism powers relentless growth, dismantling old institutions in favor of new ones — but at environmental cost. Each policy shift and technological breakthrough nudges the planet closer to ecological limits that productivity alone cannot erase.

Given visible climate damage, one might expect leaders to impose stricter guardrails. Instead, many defer action, either underestimating the crisis or trusting future science to compensate for present excess.

chapters.title: Humanism may not survive the algorithmic century
chapters.text:
Harari defines humanism as any worldview that treats humanity as the ultimate source of meaning. Liberal humanism in particular assumes individuals possess enough wisdom to choose well — a premise underpinning free elections and consumer markets.

Yet Harari challenges free will as illusion. The mind is a network of neurons translating sensation into emotion and action, often before conscious deliberation catches up. He distinguishes the experiencing self, which records moments, from the narrative self, which stitches those moments into stories and major decisions — frequently smoothing away inconvenient details and overweighting peaks and troughs.

Life aims to live it, taste experience to the utmost, and reach out eagerly and without fear for a newer and richer experience. ~ Eleanor Roosevelt

Beliefs and choices therefore depend on which experiences survive editing in memory.

Liberalism may falter as non-organic intelligence rivals human cognition. Automation already displaces workers across sectors, and Harari expects broader replacement — yet he stops short of declaring human individuality obsolete. People will likely retain roles machines cannot fully occupy.

chapters.title: Techno-humanism and Dataism may define the third millennium
chapters.text:
Harari identifies two emerging belief systems for a hyper-technological age. Techno-humanism seeks to upgrade Homo sapiens into Homo deus through biological and cognitive enhancement — continuing the long genetic journey that produced modern humans in the first place.

It is not the strongest or the most intelligent who will survive but those who can best manage change. ~ Leon C. Megginson

The risk is that enhancements designed to expand capability could erode authentic agency. Nanotechnology and bionic implants may reshape mental states so profoundly that users become dependent on the very tools meant to liberate them.

Dataism offers a different answer: humans lack the processing bandwidth to govern a data-saturated world, so authority should migrate to intelligent systems capable of analyzing information at scale. Governments and corporations already appear overwhelmed, delegating more decisions to algorithms.

Dataism is not anti-human. Rather, it reframes the ancient religious question of how to distinguish good from evil — now entrusted to computational pattern recognition.

Did you know? Dataism as a term was first used in a 2013 New York Times piece by David Brooks.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Harari is optimistic about humanity's capacity to adapt, with technology as the dominant force shaping what comes next. Change is constant; civilizations endure by revising explanations when old ones fail. Religion, in his framing, often functions as either an account of transformation or an escape from confronting it.

New belief systems will keep appearing. Science and technology increasingly perform the role earlier religions held — provoking resistance even as they deliver measurable progress. Harari contends that scientific inquiry is uniquely capable of sustaining development over the long run.

Survival has always depended on adapting to — and often dominating — the environment. Many models that served past centuries will be discarded as humanity enters a new millennium empowered to shed biological and cognitive limitations in pursuit of divinity.

When war, famine, and epidemic recede as primary threats, entirely different dangers will take their place.

We do not become satisfied by leading a peaceful and prosperous existence. Rather, we become satisfied when reality matches our expectations. The bad news is that as conditions improve, expectations balloon. ~ Prof. Yuval Noah Harari

Try this

Technology holds great promise in just about every facet of human existence; medicine, sports, and even housing. It would be beneficial if we get acquainted with the religion of dataism. Start by learning all you can about data and how it shapes the world.
